summary: Gruber analyzes Ben Thompson's argument that Apple's declining iPhone sales in China stem from WeChat's dominance as the country's de facto operating system, which neutralizes iOS's platform lock-in advantage. While agreeing with Thompson's core thesis about WeChat, Gruber pushes back on the secondary argument that the iPhone 7's familiar form factor hurts sales in status-conscious China. He argues the real problem is entirely about software loyalty: if WeChat is the platform that matters, not iOS, then Apple loses the 'hardware differentiated by software' formula that drives its entire business. The retention data is stark — only 50% of Chinese iPhone owners stayed with iPhone, versus mid-80s elsewhere. Gruber concludes this is a far more serious and intractable problem than boring hardware, because Apple can ship exciting new hardware but cannot easily displace WeChat's centrality.
key_points: Apple's entire business model rests on 'hardware differentiated by superior, exclusive software' — and WeChat breaks this formula in China by becoming the platform layer that matters most
iPhone retention rates in China have plummeted to around 50%, a dramatic outlier compared to mid-80s retention in the US, UK, and Germany
Gruber disagrees with Thompson's secondary point that the iPhone 7's similar-to-6 form factor hurts China sales, arguing this 'boring hardware' narrative is gadget-press hogwash
The WeChat problem has no easy fix: Apple can release exciting new hardware, but cannot easily reclaim platform loyalty when a third-party app is the true operating system
Gruber's thought experiment — would you rather run iOS on a Pixel or Android on an iPhone — illustrates that software platform loyalty, not hardware, is what retains customers
money_quotes: Hardware differentiated by superior, exclusive software is the key to understanding Apple. It's the reason the company was founded.
Any comparison between Microsoft's Surface Laptop and Apple's MacBooks that doesn't place heavy emphasis on the value of MacOS is vapid.
Or, to put it another way, the operating system of China is WeChat, not iOS/Android.
If forced to choose, I would much rather run iOS on a Google Pixel than Android on an iPhone 7.

In Apple's 'hardware differentiated by software' formula, the software is more important than the hardware. That's why gadget writers so often get Apple wrong: they're focused solely on hardware — the object, not the experience of using the object.
I just think the whole 'if it doesn't have an altogether new form factor, it's boring' thing is hogwash.
If it really is true that 'the operating system of China is WeChat, not iOS/Android', that's the whole ballgame right there.

If the problem is that iOS engenders far less platform loyalty in China because of WeChat's centrality — or even worse, if WeChat is central and better on Android than it is on iOS — there's no easy fix for Apple.
key_insight: WeChat's dominance as China's true operating system neutralizes Apple's core competitive advantage — software-driven platform loyalty — creating a structural problem that no amount of exciting new hardware can solve.
